planting.note: Soak dried corms in water for 4 hours before planting; plant 2 inches deep with the pointed side down (or flat side down if unsure — they are forgiving). In zones 8–11, plant in fall for winter and spring blooms. In zones 4–7, plant in early spring for summer bloom.

directions: Plant soaked corms 2 inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; florist anemone is one of the most prized cut flowers of the Mediterranean winter and spring, producing brilliant 2–3 inch poppy-like flowers in an exceptional range of colors — deep crimson, clear red, violet, pink, and white, all with dramatic black stamens in the center. The De Caen series (single) and St. Brigid series (semi-double) are the most popular cultivars for home cutting gardens. In Gulf Coast and Deep South gardens (zones 8‑9), plant corms in October or November for winter to spring blooms; temperatures above 75°F send plants into dormancy. The corms are inexpensive and can be planted in masses for a spectacular floral display.
sources: Brent and Becky's Bulbs cultural guide

planting.note: Soak claws (tubers) in water for 3–4 hours before planting; plant claw-side down, 2 inches deep, 6 inches apart. In zones 8–11, plant in October–November for winter and spring bloom. In zones 4–7, plant in early spring as soon as soil can be worked.

directions: Plant soaked claw-shaped tubers in well-drained, cool soil in full sun; Persian buttercup is the cream of the cool-season cut flower garden, producing fully double flowers 2–4 inches across with dozens of silky petals arranged in concentric layers in every color of the rainbow — cream, yellow, gold, orange, red, pink, magenta, purple, and white, many with picotee edges. They are extraordinarily beautiful as cut flowers, lasting 7–10 days in a vase. In zone 8‑9, the October-to-spring season is ideal — plant in fall, enjoy blooms from January through April. Plants go dormant in summer heat and can be left in the ground in well-drained sites or dug and stored dry. Modern Cloni and Elegance series produce the most enormous, peony-like flowers.
sources: Brent and Becky's Bulbs cultural guide

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring 4–6 weeks before last frost, or in fall for spring germination; corn poppies need cold stratification and will not germinate in warm soil. In zones 8–11, sow in fall for a brilliant spring display. Mix seed with sand for even distribution.

directions: Broadcast seed directly on bare, disturbed soil in fall or early spring — corn poppy requires light to germinate and should not be covered; simply press seed firmly into the soil. It is an archaeological companion plant that has followed grain cultivation for thousands of years, colonizing disturbed soil alongside wheat and rye. The classic scarlet red poppies with black centers are the symbol of Remembrance Day; cottage garden strains offer apricot, salmon, pink, white, and bicolors. Plants self-sow abundantly once established and will return every year. Do not transplant — sow where plants are to flower. In Gulf Coast gardens, fall sowing gives the best display, with blooms from March through May before summer heat ends the season.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in well-drained soil; star of Bethlehem naturalizes aggressively and is best planted in informal settings or rough grass where spreading is welcome. CAUTION: all parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant small bulbs in fall in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; star of Bethlehem is a Mediterranean bulb with narrow, white-striped leaves and clusters of pure white, star-shaped flowers with distinctive green stripes on the backs of the petals in late spring. It naturalizes abundantly in lawns, meadows, and rough grass, producing a carpet of white in late spring before the foliage disappears in summer. It is one of the most adaptable and independent bulbs available, thriving in partial shade, poor soils, and dry summer conditions with zero care once established. CAUTION: all parts are poisonous if eaten — not for edible gardens. The genus includes species used medicinally (with proper processing) but direct ingestion is dangerous.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck just at or above the soil surface in late summer or early fall; like many South African autumn bulbs, nerines resent disturbance and bloom most freely when crowded in a single spot. Do not divide until absolutely necessary.

directions: Plant bulbs with necks at soil surface in sharply drained soil at the base of a warm, south-facing wall; the Guernsey Lily blooms in autumn after summer dormancy, sending up clusters of 8–12 spectacular rose-pink flowers with reflexed, wavy-edged petals on bare stems before the strap leaves appear. Each floret has a sparkling, iridescent quality — the petal surfaces catch light in a way that gives the flowers an almost metallic sheen. They make superb, long-lasting cut flowers. The name refers to their long cultivation in the Channel Islands, where they naturalized on a sandy beach. Nerines thrive with summer drought and perform best against a sunny wall in the driest, warmest spot in the garden. Hardy to about 20°F once established.
